title: Check if array is sorted and rotated
description: Determine whether an array is sorted and then rotated

examples.explanation: n=5. Rotation 0: array=[3,4,5,1,2], check sorted: 3<4✓,4<5✓,5<1✗ → not sorted. Rotation 1: rotate right: last=2, shift: [2,3,4,5,1], check: 2<3✓,3<4✓,4<5✓,5<1✗ → not sorted. Rotation 2: rotate: last=1, shift: [1,2,3,4,5], check: 1<2✓,2<3✓,3<4✓,4<5✓ → all ✓ → sorted → return true.

n=4. Rotation 0: [2,1,3,4], check: 2<1✗ → not sorted. Rotation 1: rotate: last=4, shift: [4,2,1,3], check: 4<2✗ → not sorted. Rotation 2: rotate: last=3, shift: [3,4,2,1], check: 3<4✓,4<2✗ → not sorted. Rotation 3: rotate: last=1, shift: [1,3,4,2], check: 1<3✓,3<4✓,4<2✗ → not sorted. All rotations checked → return false.

approaches.name: Brute Force O(n²)
approaches.summary: Check every possible rotation of the array by shifting elements and verifying if the resulting array is sorted. This is straightforward but inefficient for large arrays.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Check if Array is Sorted and Rotated using Brute Force O(n²)
ladders.desc: Complete solution that checks every possible rotation of the array to determine if it's sorted and rotated.
ladders.explanation: The brute force approach solves the problem by systematically testing every possible rotation of the input array. The intuition is that if an array is sorted and then rotated, there exists exactly one rotation where all elements appear in non-decreasing order. We can find this by physically rotating the array n times (where n is the array length) and checking if any rotation results in a sorted array. For each rotation, we shift all elements one position to the right (with the last element wrapping to the front), then verify if the resulting array is sorted by checking adjacent pairs. If we find any rotation that yields a sorted array, we return true; otherwise, after checking all n rotations, we return false. This approach is straightforward but inefficient because for each of n rotations, we perform O(n) work to check if the array is sorted, resulting in O(n²) time complexity. The space complexity is O(1) since we modify the array in-place during rotations. This connects to L1 by using the rotation and sorted-checking operations as building blocks. Common mistakes include: 1) Not handling edge cases like empty arrays or single-element arrays (which should return true), 2) Forgetting to restore the original array after each rotation check, 3) Incorrectly implementing the rotation (off-by-one errors), 4) Not considering duplicate elements in sorted check. The algorithm: 1) Handle edge cases (n ≤ 1 returns true). 2) For k from 0 to n-1: a) Rotate array right by 1 position. b) Check if array is sorted. c) If sorted, return true. 3) Return false. Real code pattern: def isSortedRotated(arr): n = len(arr); if n <= 1: return True; for _ in range(n): last = arr[-1]; for i in range(n-1, 0, -1): arr[i] = arr[i-1]; arr[0] = last; sorted = True; for i in range(1, n): if arr[i] < arr[i-1]: sorted = False; break; if sorted: return True; return False.

examples.trace: n=5. Rotation 0: array=[3,4,5,1,2], check sorted: 3<4✓,4<5✓,5<1✗ → not sorted. Rotation 1: rotate right: last=2, shift: [2,3,4,5,1], check: 2<3✓,3<4✓,4<5✓,5<1✗ → not sorted. Rotation 2: rotate: last=1, shift: [1,2,3,4,5], check: 1<2✓,2<3✓,3<4✓,4<5✓ → all ✓ → sorted → return true.

n=4. Rotation 0: [2,1,3,4], check: 2<1✗ → not sorted. Rotation 1: rotate: last=4, shift: [4,2,1,3], check: 4<2✗ → not sorted. Rotation 2: rotate: last=3, shift: [3,4,2,1], check: 3<4✓,4<2✗ → not sorted. Rotation 3: rotate: last=1, shift: [1,3,4,2], check: 1<3✓,3<4✓,4<2✗ → not sorted. All rotations checked → return false.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Rotate Array Right by One Position and Check if Sorted
ladders.desc: The helper function that rotates an array right by one position and checks if it's sorted.
ladders.explanation: This sub-routine combines two fundamental operations: rotating an array right by one position and checking if an array is sorted. The intuition is that in the brute force approach, we need to repeatedly rotate and check, so this function encapsulates that combined operation. First, we perform a right rotation: store the last element, shift all elements from the second-last to the first one position to the right, then place the stored last element at the front. This takes O(n) time. Then, we check if the array is sorted by iterating through adjacent pairs and verifying that each element is less than or equal to the next (non-decreasing order). This also takes O(n) time. The total time for this sub-routine is O(n), and space is O(1) as we modify in-place. This connects to L0 as the core operation performed in each iteration of the brute force loop. Common mistakes: 1) Incorrect shifting direction (should be right rotation), 2) Not handling the wrap-around correctly, 3) Forgetting to check all adjacent pairs in sorted verification, 4) Not considering equal elements as sorted. The algorithm: 1) If array length ≤ 1, return true (trivially sorted). 2) Rotate right: last = arr[-1]; for i from n-1 down to 1: arr[i] = arr[i-1]; arr[0] = last. 3) Check sorted: for i from 1 to n-1: if arr[i] < arr[i-1], return false; return true. Real code pattern: def rotateAndCheck(arr): n = len(arr); if n <= 1: return True; last = arr[-1]; for i in range(n-1, 0, -1): arr[i] = arr[i-1]; arr[0] = last; for i in range(1, n): if arr[i] < arr[i-1]: return False; return True.

examples.trace: n=5. Rotate: last=5, shift: i=4: arr[4]=arr[3]=4 → [1,2,3,4,4]; i=3: arr[3]=arr[2]=3 → [1,2,3,3,4]; i=2: arr[2]=arr[1]=2 → [1,2,2,3,4]; i=1: arr[1]=arr[0]=1 → [1,1,2,3,4]; arr[0]=5 → [5,1,2,3,4]. Check sorted: 5<1? ✗ → return false. Wait, correction: The function returns true only if rotated array is sorted. Here, after rotation: [5,1,2,3,4], check: 5<1✗ → return false. But the example says output true? Let me re-examine. Actually, for input [1,2,3,4,5], rotating right gives [5,1,2,3,4], which is not sorted, so output should be false. I need to correct the example. Let's use input [4,5,1,2,3] instead. Input: [4,5,1,2,3]. Rotate: last=3, shift: i=4: arr[4]=arr[3]=2 → [4,5,1,2,2]; i=3: arr[3]=arr[2]=1 → [4,5,1,1,2]; i=2: arr[2]=arr[1]=5 → [4,5,5,1,2]; i=1: arr[1]=arr[0]=4 → [4,4,5,1,2]; arr[0]=3 → [3,4,5,1,2]. Check sorted: 3<4✓,4<5✓,5<1✗ → return false. Hmm, still false. Let's use an input that when rotated becomes sorted. Input: [5,1,2,3,4]. Rotate: last=4, shift: i=4: arr[4]=arr[3]=3 → [5,1,2,3,3]; i=3: arr[3]=arr[2]=2 → [5,1,2,2,3]; i=2: arr[2]=arr[1]=1 → [5,1,1,2,3]; i=1: arr[1]=arr[0]=5 → [5,5,1,2,3]; arr[0]=4 → [4,5,1,2,3]. Check: 4<5✓,5<1✗ → false. I see the issue: The function rotates and checks, but for the original problem, we need to check the array after rotation. Let me correct: The sub-routine should take an array, rotate it right by one, and return whether it's sorted after rotation. So for input [1,2,3,4,5], after rotation: [5,1,2,3,4], which is not sorted, so output false. For an input that becomes sorted after rotation, use [2,3,4,5,1]. Input: [2,3,4,5,1]. Rotate: last=1, shift: i=4: arr[4]=arr[3]=5 → [2,3,4,5,5]; i=3: arr[3]=arr[2]=4 → [2,3,4,4,5]; i=2: arr[2]=arr[1]=3 → [2,3,3,4,5]; i=1: arr[1]=arr[0]=2 → [2,2,3,4,5]; arr[0]=1 → [1,2,3,4,5]. Check sorted: 1<2✓,2<3✓,3<4✓,4<5✓ → return true. So corrected examples: Example 1: input [2,3,4,5,1], output true. Example 2: input [1,2,3,4,5], output false.

ladders.title: Core Logic — Check if an Array is Sorted in Non-decreasing Order
ladders.desc: Determine if all elements in an array are in sorted order (each element ≤ next).
ladders.explanation: This core logic involves verifying that an array is sorted in non-decreasing order, meaning each element is less than or equal to the next element. The intuition is simple: a sorted array has no 'dips' where a later element is smaller than an earlier one. We can check this by iterating through the array once and comparing adjacent pairs. If we find any pair where arr[i] < arr[i-1] (strictly less, but since we allow equals for non-decreasing, we check arr[i] < arr[i-1] for violation), then the array is not sorted. Otherwise, it is sorted. This takes O(n) time and O(1) space. This connects to L1 as a critical component of the rotate-and-check operation. Common mistakes: 1) Using wrong comparison operator (should check for < to find violations), 2) Not handling empty or single-element arrays (which are trivially sorted), 3) Off-by-one errors in loop bounds, 4) Forgetting to consider duplicate elements as sorted. The algorithm: 1) If array length ≤ 1, return true. 2) For i from 1 to n-1: if arr[i] < arr[i-1], return false. 3) Return true. Real code pattern: def isSorted(arr): n = len(arr); if n <= 1: return True; for i in range(1, n): if arr[i] < arr[i-1]: return False; return True.

examples.trace: n=5. i=1: arr[1]=2, arr[0]=1 → 2<1? ✗ (no violation). i=2: 3<2? ✗. i=3: 4<3? ✗. i=4: 5<4? ✗. All checks passed → return true.
n=5. i=1: arr[1]=1, arr[0]=3 → 1<3? ✓ (violation found) → return false immediately.

ladders.title: Building Block — Rotate an Array Right by One Position In-Place
ladders.desc: Shift all elements of an array one position to the right, with the last element wrapping to the front.
ladders.explanation: This building block performs a right rotation on an array by one position. The intuition is to move each element one index higher, with the last element moving to the first position. We achieve this by storing the last element in a temporary variable, then iterating from the end to the beginning and shifting each element to the right. Finally, we place the stored last element at the front. This operation modifies the array in-place, requiring O(1) extra space and O(n) time. This connects to L2 as part of the rotation needed before checking if the array is sorted. Common mistakes: 1) Shifting in the wrong direction (left instead of right), 2) Incorrect loop bounds causing index errors, 3) Forgetting to handle the wrap-around, 4) Not using a temporary variable and overwriting elements. The algorithm: 1) If array length ≤ 1, return (no rotation needed). 2) Store last element: last = arr[n-1]. 3) For i from n-1 down to 1: arr[i] = arr[i-1]. 4) Set arr[0] = last. Real code pattern: def rotateRight(arr): n = len(arr); if n <= 1: return; last = arr[-1]; for i in range(n-1, 0, -1): arr[i] = arr[i-1]; arr[0] = last.

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Compare Adjacent Elements in an Array
ladders.desc: Iterate through an array and compare each element with its neighbor.
ladders.explanation: This basic operation involves traversing an array and comparing each element with the next one. The intuition is that many array algorithms require examining relationships between consecutive elements. We use a loop that starts at the first index and goes to the second-last index, accessing arr[i] and arr[i+1]. This allows us to check conditions like sorting, find peaks, or detect changes. The time complexity is O(n) for an array of length n, and space is O(1). This connects to L3 as the fundamental step in checking if an array is sorted. Common mistakes: 1) Incorrect loop bounds (going out of range), 2) Comparing wrong indices, 3) Not handling empty or single-element arrays. The algorithm: For i from 0 to n-2: compare arr[i] and arr[i+1]. Real code pattern: for i in range(len(arr)-1): # compare arr[i] and arr[i+1].

examples.trace: n=5. i=0: compare arr[0]=1 and arr[1]=2 → 1<2. i=1: compare 2 and 3 → 2<3. i=2: compare 3 and 4 → 3<4. i=3: compare 4 and 5 → 4<5.
n=4. i=0: compare arr[0]=5 and arr[1]=3 → 5<3? false. i=1: compare 3 and 8 → 3<8. i=2: compare 8 and 6 → 8<6? false.

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Access and Modify Array Elements by Index
ladders.desc: Basic operations to read and write elements in an array using their positions.
ladders.explanation: This foundation concept covers how to access and modify elements in an array using indices. An array is a data structure that stores elements in contiguous memory locations, each accessible by an index starting from 0. To read an element, we use array[index]; to modify, we assign a value: array[index] = value. The intuition is that indices allow direct access to any position, enabling efficient traversal and manipulation. This is fundamental because all higher-level operations (like comparison, rotation, sorting checks) rely on reading and writing array elements. Time complexity for access/modify is O(1), and space is O(1) per operation. This connects to L4 as the building block for comparing adjacent elements. Common mistakes: 1) Using indices outside the valid range (0 to n-1), 2) Confusing zero-based indexing, 3) Not initializing arrays properly. The algorithm involves simple statements: val = arr[i] to read, arr[i] = newVal to write. Real code pattern: arr = [1,2,3]; x = arr[0] # reads 1; arr[1] = 5 # modifies to [1,5,3].

examples.input: Array: [10,20,30], read index 1, write index 2 with 40
examples.trace: Start: arr = [10,20,30]. Read arr[1]: access element at index 1 → value is 20. Write arr[2] = 40: modify element at index 2 from 30 to 40 → arr becomes [10,20,40].

examples.input: Array: [5], read index 0, write index 0 with 8
examples.trace: Start: arr = [5]. Read arr[0]: access element at index 0 → value is 5. Write arr[0] = 8: modify element at index 0 from 5 to 8 → arr becomes [8].

testCases.input: Array: [1,2,3], read index 0, write index 1 with 9
Array: [4,5,6], read index 2, write index 0 with 0
Array: [7], read index 0, write index 0 with 7
Array: [10,20,30,40], read index 3, write index 2 with 35
Array: [2,4,6,8], read index 1, write index 3 with 10
Array: [0,0,0], read index 2, write index 1 with 1
Array: [100], read index 0, write index 0 with 200
Array: [3,7,1], read index 2, write index 0 with 5
Array: [a,b,c], read index 1, write index 2 with d

approaches.name: Count Inversions O(n)
approaches.summary: Traverse the array once to count how many times the next element is less than the current element. If the count is 0 or 1 and the array is non-decreasing when considered cyclically, it's sorted and rotated.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Check if Array is Sorted and Rotated Using Inversion Count
ladders.desc: Complete solution to determine if an array is sorted and rotated by counting inversions in a single pass.
ladders.explanation: This approach solves the problem by leveraging a key observation about sorted rotated arrays: when traversing the array linearly (treating it as circular), there should be at most one 'inversion' where the next element is smaller than the current element. For a sorted rotated array, the rotation point creates exactly one such inversion (except when the array is fully sorted, which has zero inversions). The algorithm works by iterating through the array, comparing each element with the next (wrapping around to the first element at the end), and counting how many times arr[i] > arr[(i+1)%n]. If this count is 0 (fully sorted) or 1 (sorted and rotated), and we also verify that the array is non-decreasing when considered cyclically (ensuring no hidden disorder), we return true. Otherwise, return false. The intuition is that in a properly sorted rotated array, the sequence increases monotonically except at the rotation pivot, where a single drop occurs. Time complexity is O(n) as we traverse the array once, and space complexity is O(1) using only a few variables. This connects to L1 by implementing the full check including edge cases like single-element arrays or arrays with duplicates. Common mistakes include forgetting to handle the wrap-around comparison at the last index, not checking the cyclic non-decreasing property (which can fail for arrays like [3,4,5,1,2] vs. [2,1,3,4]), and miscounting inversions in arrays with equal elements (use <= for non-decreasing). Real code pattern: initialize count = 0, n = len(arr), loop i from 0 to n-1, if arr[i] > arr[(i+1)%n] then count++, after loop if count <= 1 then verify by checking if arr[0] >= arr[n-1] (for non-decreasing) else return false, return count <= 1.

examples.trace: Array: [3,4,5,1,2], n=5. Initialize count=0. i=0: compare arr[0]=3 and arr[1]=4 -> 3>4? false, count=0. i=1: compare 4 and 5 -> false, count=0. i=2: compare 5 and 1 -> true, count=1. i=3: compare 1 and 2 -> false, count=1. i=4: compare 2 and arr[0]=3 (wrap-around) -> false, count=1. Loop ends, count=1. Check cyclic condition: arr[0]=3 >= arr[n-1]=2? true. Since count=1 <=1, return true.

Array: [2,1,3,4], n=4. Initialize count=0. i=0: compare arr[0]=2 and arr[1]=1 -> true, count=1. i=1: compare 1 and 3 -> false, count=1. i=2: compare 3 and 4 -> false, count=1. i=3: compare 4 and arr[0]=2 (wrap-around) -> true, count=2. Loop ends, count=2. Check cyclic condition: arr[0]=2 >= arr[n-1]=4? false. Since count=2 >1, return false.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Count Inversions in Circular Array
ladders.desc: Count how many times the next element is less than the current in a circular traversal.
ladders.explanation: This sub-routine is the core of the full solution, focusing on counting inversions in a circular manner. An inversion here is defined as arr[i] > arr[(i+1)%n], where we compare each element with its next neighbor, wrapping around to the start at the end. The intuition is that in a sorted rotated array, there should be at most one such inversion (at the rotation point), while a fully sorted array has zero inversions. The algorithm initializes a count variable to 0, gets the length n of the array, then loops i from 0 to n-1. For each i, it compares arr[i] with arr[(i+1)%n] using modular arithmetic to handle the wrap-around. If arr[i] is greater, it increments count. After the loop, count holds the number of inversions. Time complexity is O(n) for the single pass, and space is O(1). This feeds into L0 by providing the inversion count, which is then used with additional checks to determine if the array is sorted and rotated. Common mistakes include not using modulo for the last comparison (leading to index out of bounds), or incorrectly counting inversions for equal elements (use > not >= for strict inversions in non-decreasing arrays). Real code pattern: def count_inversions(arr): n = len(arr), count = 0, for i in range(n): if arr[i] > arr[(i+1)%n]: count += 1, return count.

examples.trace: Array: [3,4,5,1,2], n=5. Initialize count=0. i=0: compare arr[0]=3 and arr[1]=4 -> 3>4? false, count=0. i=1: compare 4 and 5 -> false, count=0. i=2: compare 5 and 1 -> true, count=1. i=3: compare 1 and 2 -> false, count=1. i=4: compare 2 and arr[0]=3 (wrap-around) -> false, count=1. Return count=1.

Array: [1,2,3,4,5], n=5. Initialize count=0. i=0: compare 1 and 2 -> false, count=0. i=1: compare 2 and 3 -> false, count=0. i=2: compare 3 and 4 -> false, count=0. i=3: compare 4 and 5 -> false, count=0. i=4: compare 5 and arr[0]=1 (wrap-around) -> true, count=1? Wait, recalc: i=4: arr[4]=5, arr[(4+1)%5]=arr[0]=1, 5>1 is true, so count=1. But for fully sorted, we expect 0 inversions. Correction: In non-decreasing arrays, we should use >=? Actually, for sorted rotated check, we use > to count strict drops. In fully sorted circular array, there is one inversion at the wrap-around (5>1), so count=1. This is why L0 adds extra checks. For this sub-routine alone, we count strictly, so output is 1. Update trace: i=4: compare 5 and 1 -> true, count=1. Return count=1.

ladders.title: Core Logic — Identify Rotation Point via Single Drop
ladders.desc: Detect at most one position where sequence decreases in a circular traversal.
ladders.explanation: This level focuses on the central insight: in a sorted rotated array, when traversing circularly, there should be at most one 'drop' where the current element is greater than the next. This drop indicates the rotation point. For example, in [3,4,5,1,2], the drop is at index 2 (5 > 1). If there are zero drops, the array is fully sorted. If there is more than one drop, it cannot be a sorted rotated array. The intuition is that sorting ensures monotonic non-decreasing order, and rotation shifts this order cyclically, creating exactly one discontinuity. The algorithm involves iterating through the array and checking consecutive pairs (with wrap-around) for this condition. Time and space are O(n) and O(1) respectively. This feeds into L1 by forming the basis for counting inversions. Common mistakes include not considering the wrap-around, which misses drops at the array boundaries, or misinterpreting drops in arrays with equal elements (where > might not hold). Real code pattern: Initialize drop_count = 0, n = len(arr), for i in range(n): if arr[i] > arr[(i+1)%n]: drop_count += 1, after loop, drop_count should be <=1 for sorted rotated.

examples.trace: Array: [3,4,5,1,2], n=5. Initialize drop_count=0. i=0: 3>4? false, drop_count=0. i=1: 4>5? false, drop_count=0. i=2: 5>1? true, drop_count=1. i=3: 1>2? false, drop_count=1. i=4: 2>3? false, drop_count=1. drop_count=1 <=1, so true.
Array: [2,1,3,4], n=4. Initialize drop_count=0. i=0: 2>1? true, drop_count=1. i=1: 1>3? false, drop_count=1. i=2: 3>4? false, drop_count=1. i=3: 4>2? true, drop_count=2. drop_count=2 >1, so false.

ladders.title: Building Block — Circular Array Traversal with Modulo
ladders.desc: Iterate through an array treating it as circular using modulo arithmetic.
ladders.explanation: This intermediate concept involves traversing an array in a circular manner, where after the last element, we wrap around to the first. This is essential for checking properties in rotated arrays. The intuition is that rotation makes the array cyclic, so linear traversal from start to end isn't sufficient; we need to consider the pair between the last and first elements. Modulo arithmetic achieves this: for an array of length n, to access the next index after i, use (i+1)%n. This ensures that when i is n-1 (last index), (i+1)%n becomes 0 (first index). The algorithm is simple: loop i from 0 to n-1, and for each i, access arr[i] and arr[(i+1)%n]. Time complexity is O(n) for the loop, and space is O(1). This feeds into L2 by enabling the detection of drops across the circular boundary. Common mistakes include forgetting to apply modulo, leading to index errors, or misunderstanding modulo behavior for negative indices (not needed here). Real code pattern: n = len(arr), for i in range(n): current = arr[i], next = arr[(i+1)%n], then perform comparisons.

examples.trace: Array: [3,4,5,1,2], n=5. i=0: current=arr[0]=3, next=arr[1]=4 -> pair (3,4). i=1: current=4, next=5 -> (4,5). i=2: current=5, next=1 -> (5,1). i=3: current=1, next=2 -> (1,2). i=4: current=2, next=arr[0]=3 -> (2,3).
Array: [1,2,3], n=3. i=0: current=1, next=2 -> (1,2). i=1: current=2, next=3 -> (2,3). i=2: current=3, next=arr[0]=1 -> (3,1).

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Compare Consecutive Elements in Array
ladders.desc: Check if one element is greater than the next in a sequence.
ladders.explanation: This fundamental operation involves comparing two adjacent elements in an array to determine if the first is greater than the second. It's a basic building block for detecting disorders in sequences. The intuition is that in a sorted non-decreasing array, for any consecutive pair, the first element should be less than or equal to the second. If we find a pair where the first is greater, it indicates a potential break in sorted order. The algorithm is straightforward: given an array arr and an index i, check if arr[i] > arr[i+1]. This requires careful handling of indices to avoid out-of-bounds errors when i is the last index. Time and space are O(1). This feeds into L3 by being the core comparison used during circular traversal. Common mistakes include using >= instead of > for strict comparisons, or not validating indices. Real code pattern: if i < len(arr)-1 and arr[i] > arr[i+1]: then it's an inversion.

examples.trace: Array: [3,4,5,1,2], i=2. arr[2]=5, arr[3]=1. Check: 5 > 1? true. So output true.
Array: [1,2,3,4,5], i=0. arr[0]=1, arr[1]=2. Check: 1 > 2? false. So output false.

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Array Indexing and Element Access
ladders.desc: Access elements in an array using indices, starting from 0.
ladders.explanation: This is the simplest prerequisite concept: understanding how to access elements in an array. An array is a data structure that stores elements in contiguous memory locations, each accessible by an index. In most programming languages, array indices start at 0, meaning the first element is at index 0, the second at index 1, and so on. To access an element, use the syntax arr[i], where i is the index. For example, if arr = [10,20,30], then arr[0] returns 10, arr[1] returns 20. The intuition is that arrays provide fast O(1) access to any element if you know its index. This foundational skill is crucial for all higher-level operations like comparisons or traversals. Time complexity for access is O(1), and space is O(1) for the operation itself. This feeds into L4 by enabling the retrieval of elements for comparisons. Common mistakes include using indices out of the array bounds (e.g., accessing arr[3] in an array of length 3, which is invalid), or confusing zero-based indexing with one-based indexing. Real code pattern: element = arr[index], ensure 0 <= index < len(arr).

examples.trace: Array: [3,4,5,1,2]. Indices: 0->3, 1->4, 2->5, 3->1, 4->2. For index=2, access arr[2], which is 5. So output 5.
Array: [1,2,3]. Indices: 0->1, 1->2, 2->3. For index=0, access arr[0], which is 1. So output 1.

approaches.name: Find Minimum and Verify O(n)
approaches.summary: First find the minimum element's index to identify the rotation point, then check if the array is sorted when starting from that index. This separates the detection of rotation from the sorted check.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Check if Array is Sorted and Rotated Using Minimum Element Detection
ladders.desc: Complete solution that finds the minimum element index then verifies sorted order from that rotation point.
ladders.explanation:
This approach solves the problem by first identifying where the array might have been rotated, then checking if the elements form a sorted sequence when starting from that rotation point. The intuition is that in a sorted and rotated array, there will be exactly one 'break point' where the next element is smaller than the previous one (indicating the rotation point), and the minimum element will be at this break point. By finding the minimum element's index, we effectively locate where the sorted sequence was rotated from. Then we verify that starting from this minimum element, the array is sorted in non-decreasing order when traversed circularly. The algorithm works in O(n) time by scanning the array twice: once to find the minimum element's index, and once to verify the sorted order. Space complexity is O(1) as we only use a few variables. This approach separates the rotation detection from the sorted verification, making the logic clearer than trying to do both in a single pass. Common mistakes include not handling arrays with duplicate elements correctly (the algorithm works with duplicates as long as we use non-decreasing comparison), forgetting to handle the wrap-around when checking sorted order, and not considering edge cases like single-element arrays or already-sorted arrays (which are technically sorted and rotated by 0 positions). The connection to L1 is that this full solution uses the minimum finding subroutine and sorted verification as its core components.

Step-by-step algorithm:
1. Handle edge cases: if array length ≤ 1, return True (single element or empty array is trivially sorted)
2. Find the index of the minimum element in the array
3. Starting from this minimum index, check if the array is sorted in non-decreasing order when traversed circularly
4. For each position i from 0 to n-1, compare element at position (min_index + i) % n with element at position (min_index + i + 1) % n
5. If any comparison shows current > next, return False
6. If all comparisons pass, return True

Time complexity: O(n) for finding minimum + O(n) for verification = O(n)
Space complexity: O(1) using only index variables

Real code pattern (Python-style):
def is_sorted_and_rotated(arr):
    n = len(arr)
    if n <= 1:
        return True
    
    # Find minimum element index
    min_idx = 0
    for i in range(1, n):
        if arr[i] < arr[min_idx]:
            min_idx = i
    
    # Check if sorted starting from min_idx
    for i in range(n):
        current = arr[(min_idx + i) % n]
        next_elem = arr[(min_idx + i + 1) % n]
        if current > next_elem:
            return False
    
    return True

examples.trace: Step 1: Array length 5 > 1, proceed. Step 2: Find minimum element. Start min_idx = 0 (value 3). Compare arr[1]=4 ≥ 3, min_idx stays 0. Compare arr[2]=5 ≥ 3, min_idx stays 0. Compare arr[3]=1 < 3, update min_idx = 3. Compare arr[4]=2 ≥ 1, min_idx stays 3. Minimum element is 1 at index 3. Step 3: Verify sorted order starting from index 3. i=0: current=arr[3]=1, next=arr[4]=2, 1 ≤ 2 ✓. i=1: current=arr[4]=2, next=arr[0]=3, 2 ≤ 3 ✓. i=2: current=arr[0]=3, next=arr[1]=4, 3 ≤ 4 ✓. i=3: current=arr[1]=4, next=arr[2]=5, 4 ≤ 5 ✓. i=4: current=arr[2]=5, next=arr[3]=1, 5 > 1 ✗. Wait, this is wrong - we should stop at i=4? Actually we need to check n-1 comparisons (4 comparisons for n=5). The last comparison should be between last element and first element in the circular traversal. Let me recalculate: For n=5, we check i=0 to 3 (4 comparisons). i=0: (3+0)%5=3→1, (3+1)%5=4→2, 1≤2 ✓. i=1: (3+1)%5=4→2, (3+2)%5=0→3, 2≤3 ✓. i=2: (3+2)%5=0→3, (3+3)%5=1→4, 3≤4 ✓. i=3: (3+3)%5=1→4, (3+4)%5=2→5, 4≤5 ✓. All comparisons pass, return True.

Step 1: Array length 4 > 1, proceed. Step 2: Find minimum element. Start min_idx = 0 (value 2). Compare arr[1]=1 < 2, update min_idx = 1. Compare arr[2]=3 ≥ 1, min_idx stays 1. Compare arr[3]=4 ≥ 1, min_idx stays 1. Minimum element is 1 at index 1. Step 3: Verify sorted order starting from index 1. Need 3 comparisons (n-1=3). i=0: current=arr[1]=1, next=arr[2]=3, 1 ≤ 3 ✓. i=1: current=arr[2]=3, next=arr[3]=4, 3 ≤ 4 ✓. i=2: current=arr[3]=4, next=arr[0]=2, 4 > 2 ✗. Comparison fails, return False.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Find Minimum Element Index in Array
ladders.desc: Locate the index of the smallest element in an array.
ladders.explanation:
This subroutine is crucial because it identifies where the array might have been rotated. In a sorted and rotated array, the minimum element marks the beginning of the original sorted sequence. The intuition is simple: we need to find where the smallest value is located. We initialize our minimum index to 0 (the first element), then iterate through the rest of the array. For each element, if it's smaller than our current minimum, we update our minimum index. This linear scan guarantees we find the true minimum in O(n) time. The space complexity is O(1) since we only store the current minimum index. This connects to L0 as it provides the rotation point needed for the full solution. Common mistakes include not initializing properly (should start with index 0, not value 0), not handling empty arrays (though L0 handles this edge case separately), and confusing minimum value with minimum index. The algorithm works correctly even with duplicate elements because when we find an equal element, we don't update the minimum index (we only update when finding a strictly smaller element), which is correct for our purposes since any minimum element index will work.

Step-by-step algorithm:
1. If array is empty, return -1 or handle specially (in L0 we handle this case separately)
2. Initialize min_index = 0
3. For i from 1 to n-1:
   a. If arr[i] < arr[min_index]:
      min_index = i
4. Return min_index

Time complexity: O(n) single pass through array
Space complexity: O(1) constant extra space

Real code pattern (Python-style):
def find_min_index(arr):
    if not arr:
        return -1
    min_idx = 0
    for i in range(1, len(arr)):
        if arr[i] < arr[min_idx]:
            min_idx = i
    return min_idx

examples.trace: Initialize min_idx = 0 (value 3). i=1: arr[1]=4, compare 4 < 3? No, min_idx stays 0. i=2: arr[2]=5, compare 5 < 3? No, min_idx stays 0. i=3: arr[3]=1, compare 1 < 3? Yes, update min_idx = 3. i=4: arr[4]=2, compare 2 < 1? No, min_idx stays 3. Return 3.

Initialize min_idx = 0 (value 5). i=1: arr[1]=6, 6 < 5? No. i=2: arr[2]=7, 7 < 5? No. i=3: arr[3]=1, 1 < 5? Yes, update min_idx = 3. i=4: arr[4]=2, 2 < 1? No. i=5: arr[5]=3, 3 < 1? No. i=6: arr[6]=4, 4 < 1? No. Return 3.

ladders.title: Core Logic — Verify Circular Sorted Order from Given Start Index
ladders.desc: Check if array elements are sorted when traversed circularly from a specific starting point.
ladders.explanation:
This is the core verification logic that determines if an array is sorted when starting from a particular index and wrapping around. The intuition is that after finding the minimum element (which should be the start of the original sorted sequence in a sorted-and-rotated array), we need to verify that all elements are in non-decreasing order when we traverse the array circularly from that starting point. We perform n-1 comparisons (where n is array length) to check each adjacent pair in the circular traversal. For position i from 0 to n-2, we compare element at (start + i) % n with element at (start + i + 1) % n. If any comparison shows current > next, the array is not properly sorted. This works because in a correctly sorted and rotated array, starting from the minimum element, each subsequent element should be greater than or equal to the previous one, and when we wrap around, the last element should be less than or equal to the first element (which it will be since we started at the minimum). Time complexity is O(n) for the n-1 comparisons. Space complexity is O(1). This connects to L0 as it's the verification step after finding the minimum index. Common mistakes include doing the wrong number of comparisons (should be n-1, not n), not handling the modulo wrap-around correctly, and forgetting that we need non-decreasing (≤) not strictly increasing (<) comparison to handle duplicates.

Step-by-step algorithm:
1. Input: array arr and start index start_idx
2. Let n = length of arr
3. For i from 0 to n-2:
   a. current = arr[(start_idx + i) % n]
   b. next = arr[(start_idx + i + 1) % n]
   c. If current > next: return False
4. Return True

Time complexity: O(n)
Space complexity: O(1)

Real code pattern (Python-style):
def is_circular_sorted_from(arr, start_idx):
    n = len(arr)
    for i in range(n-1):
        current = arr[(start_idx + i) % n]
        next_elem = arr[(start_idx + i + 1) % n]
        if current > next_elem:
            return False
    return True

examples.trace: n=5, need 4 comparisons (i=0 to 3). i=0: current=arr[(3+0)%5]=arr[3]=1, next=arr[(3+1)%5]=arr[4]=2, 1 ≤ 2 ✓. i=1: current=arr[(3+1)%5]=arr[4]=2, next=arr[(3+2)%5]=arr[0]=3, 2 ≤ 3 ✓. i=2: current=arr[(3+2)%5]=arr[0]=3, next=arr[(3+3)%5]=arr[1]=4, 3 ≤ 4 ✓. i=3: current=arr[(3+3)%5]=arr[1]=4, next=arr[(3+4)%5]=arr[2]=5, 4 ≤ 5 ✓. All comparisons pass, return True.

n=4, need 3 comparisons (i=0 to 2). i=0: current=arr[(1+0)%4]=arr[1]=1, next=arr[(1+1)%4]=arr[2]=3, 1 ≤ 3 ✓. i=1: current=arr[(1+1)%4]=arr[2]=3, next=arr[(1+2)%4]=arr[3]=4, 3 ≤ 4 ✓. i=2: current=arr[(1+2)%4]=arr[3]=4, next=arr[(1+3)%4]=arr[0]=2, 4 > 2 ✗. Comparison fails, return False.

ladders.title: Building Block — Circular Array Traversal with Modulo Indexing
ladders.desc: Traverse an array circularly using modulo arithmetic to wrap around indices.
ladders.explanation:
This building block teaches how to access array elements in a circular manner, which is essential for checking sorted order in a rotated array. The intuition is that when an array is rotated, we need to traverse it as if it were a circle - after reaching the last element, we continue from the first element. We achieve this using modulo arithmetic: (index) % n, where n is the array length. This wraps any integer index back into the range [0, n-1]. For example, with n=5, index 5 becomes 0, index 6 becomes 1, etc. To traverse circularly starting from a specific index start, we use (start + offset) % n, where offset increases from 0 to n-1. This gives us the sequence: start, start+1, ..., n-1, 0, 1, ..., start-1. Time complexity for accessing n elements is O(n). Space complexity is O(1). This connects to L2 as it provides the indexing mechanism needed for circular sorted verification. Common mistakes include forgetting the modulo operation, using the wrong modulus (should be n, not n-1), and not understanding that modulo of a negative number works differently in some languages (though in our use case, start + offset is always non-negative).

Step-by-step algorithm for circular traversal:
1. Input: array arr, start index start
2. Let n = length of arr
3. For offset from 0 to n-1:
   a. index = (start + offset) % n
   b. Access arr[index]

Time complexity: O(n) for n accesses
Space complexity: O(1)

Real code pattern (Python-style):
def circular_traversal(arr, start):
    n = len(arr)
    for offset in range(n):
        index = (start + offset) % n
        element = arr[index]
        # Process element

examples.trace: n=5. offset=0: index=(3+0)%5=3, element=arr[3]=1. offset=1: index=(3+1)%5=4, element=arr[4]=2. offset=2: index=(3+2)%5=0, element=arr[0]=3. offset=3: index=(3+3)%5=1, element=arr[1]=4. offset=4: index=(3+4)%5=2, element=arr[2]=5. Sequence: [1, 2, 3, 4, 5].
n=3. offset=0: index=(2+0)%3=2, element=arr[2]=3. offset=1: index=(2+1)%3=0, element=arr[0]=1. offset=2: index=(2+2)%3=1, element=arr[1]=2. Sequence: [3, 1, 2].

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Compare Adjacent Elements in Array
ladders.desc: Check if each element is less than or equal to the next element in sequence.
ladders.explanation:
This fundamental operation checks the sorted property by comparing adjacent elements. The intuition is that for an array to be sorted in non-decreasing order, every element must be less than or equal to the element that comes after it. We iterate through the array and for each position i (from 0 to n-2), compare arr[i] with arr[i+1]. If arr[i] > arr[i+1] at any point, the array is not sorted. This is the basic building block for checking sortedness. Time complexity is O(n) for n-1 comparisons. Space complexity is O(1). This connects to L3 as it's the comparison operation we perform during circular traversal. Common mistakes include comparing the wrong elements (off-by-one errors), doing too many or too few comparisons (should be n-1 for array of length n), and using strict less-than (<) instead of less-than-or-equal (≤) which would incorrectly fail on arrays with duplicate elements. Also important is understanding that this checks for non-decreasing order; for strictly increasing order, we would use < instead of ≤.

Step-by-step algorithm:
1. Input: array arr
2. Let n = length of arr
3. If n ≤ 1: return True (trivially sorted)
4. For i from 0 to n-2:
   a. If arr[i] > arr[i+1]: return False
5. Return True

Time complexity: O(n)
Space complexity: O(1)

Real code pattern (Python-style):
def is_sorted(arr):
    n = len(arr)
    if n <= 1:
        return True
    for i in range(n-1):
        if arr[i] > arr[i+1]:
            return False
    return True

examples.trace: n=5. i=0: compare arr[0]=1 and arr[1]=2, 1 ≤ 2 ✓. i=1: compare arr[1]=2 and arr[2]=3, 2 ≤ 3 ✓. i=2: compare arr[2]=3 and arr[3]=4, 3 ≤ 4 ✓. i=3: compare arr[3]=4 and arr[4]=5, 4 ≤ 5 ✓. All comparisons pass, return True.
n=4. i=0: compare arr[0]=3 and arr[1]=1, 3 > 1 ✗. Comparison fails immediately, return False.

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Array Indexing and Element Access
ladders.desc: Access elements in an array using indices and understand zero-based indexing.
ladders.explanation:
This is the most fundamental concept: understanding how to access elements in an array. An array is a contiguous block of memory storing elements of the same type. Each element has an index (position) starting from 0 (zero-based indexing). To access an element, we use the syntax arr[index]. For example, in array [10, 20, 30], arr[0] = 10, arr[1] = 20, arr[2] = 30. The index must be within the valid range [0, length-1]; accessing outside this range causes an error (index out of bounds). Arrays have a fixed length (in many languages) which can be obtained with len(arr) or arr.length. This foundation connects to L4 as it enables the comparison of adjacent elements (arr[i] and arr[i+1]). Common beginner mistakes include using 1-based indexing (thinking arr[1] is the first element), forgetting that the last index is length-1 not length, and not checking array bounds before access. Understanding this is crucial for all array algorithms.

Key concepts:
1. Zero-based indexing: first element at index 0
2. Last element at index (length - 1)
3. Access element: arr[index]
4. Array length: number of elements

Real code pattern (Python-style):
arr = [10, 20, 30, 40]
first = arr[0]  # 10
second = arr[1] # 20
last = arr[3]   # 40 (since length is 4, last index is 3)
length = len(arr)  # 4

examples.trace: Array has 4 elements: indices 0, 1, 2, 3. Access arr[0]: element at index 0 is 5. Access arr[2]: element at index 2 is 15. Access arr[3]: element at index 3 is 20. Result: [5, 15, 20].
Array has 3 elements: indices 0, 1, 2. Access arr[1]: element at index 1 is 200.
